Essential Messaging Concepts

Messages can be from various sources and can be sent for a variety of purposes. The primary categories of messages are as follows.

Category Description
Publisher messages These are messages that a publisher creates externally to target users of their specific title. Think of these as classic Message of the Day messages displayed to the user when they go online with the title. Publisher messages may include text, voice, or title-specific attachments.
Title-specific messages Every title has the option of creating a unique message for use within the title. This enables developers to innovate above and beyond what's generically provided by the service.
Friend request These messages provide players with a means of asking someone to be a friend and appear on their friends list.
Game invite These types of messages are invitations from an existing friend to play a game.
Competition invite These types of messages are invitations to join a competition.
Team recruitment These types of messages are invitations to join a team.

Message Queue

Each player on the Xbox Live service is assigned a unique message queue. The queue contains messages for the individual user and is limited to 125 messages. All messages from all titles occupy the single per-player queue. The messaging API enables your title to enumerate the messages in the player's queue, display the messages associated with the current title, and delete messages from the queue. The API also enables your title to allow the player to send new messages to the queues of other players.

Message Expiration

It's important to understand that the message service is not designed as a place to permanently store data or communications. Therefore, Xbox Live implements aggressive expiration policies for messages and attachments. To reinforce this, titles are required to display the expiration date of messages as they are processed by the user. Upon expiration, the message summary, details, and all accompanying attachments are deleted from the player's message queue. This completely removes expired messages from the service.

All messages have a maximum expiration time defined by message type. The maximum expiration of any message is 45 days. Titles that implement a title-specific message may choose an expiration value that makes sense for their message type, providing it's less than 45 days.

Special expiration policies apply to Xbox Live global messages. These include system messages and publisher-sent messages. These message types have two expiration methods controlled by a flag on the message. By the first method, a global message can be set to expire on a specific date similar to normal messages in the user message queue. This could be used to send a message of the day or week. Such a message would be set to expire at the end of the specified time period, after which it is no longer applicable. By the second method, a global message can be set to begin expiring once it is first placed in the user's queue. Publishers could use this method to post welcome messages to their titles. New users logging on to those titles would receive the message, which would then expire in the selected number of days.

Cross-Title Messaging

Although the Xbox Live team is currently considering cross-title messaging, it is not supported at present. In other words, a message sent from title A can be received and processed only in title A. As a corollary, players will be sent notifications only for messages applicable to the title they are currently playing.

However, messaging can be shared between titles that use a common family title ID. If a publisher chooses to utilize a family title ID for a series of products, for example, they could share messages across those titles. See the XOnlineMessageEnableReceivingFamilyTitleIDs and XOnlineMessageSetSendingFamilyTitleID functions for more information.
